slides.title: 2b. rfc5881bis Update (BFD for IPv4 and IPv6  (Single Hop))

chat.author: John Scudder
chat.text: On the “one implementation” thing I would start by asking what specifically it’s one implementation of. Some things in BFD require interoperability in which case I’d guess it’s a no. But there are some things where you can basically do it locally without far-end cooperation, right? In which case I’d think there’s a strong case to keep it even with just 1 implementation.

chat.author: Ketan Talaulikar
chat.text: We need 2 implementations that interop for that feature to be part of the Internet Standard. That part is clear.

chat.author: Ketan Talaulikar
chat.text: If some feature does not get to that level then the question is what happens to that. And I will get back. My gut feeling is move it into a PS and let it be there.

chat.author: John Scudder
chat.text:
Ketan Talaulikar said:

If some feature does not get to that level then the question is what happens to that. And I will get back. My gut feeling is move it into a PS and let it be there.

That’s a good point. Then the WG can request advancement to Internet Standard as soon as there’s a second implementation.

chat.author: Jeffrey Haas
chat.text: Doing that for a feature in the core RFC is really the main problem. :-)

chat.author: Jeffrey Haas
chat.text: For example, demand mode. If > 2 implementations by same vendor isn't enough, then core bfd rfc doesn't advance to the full standard we're trying to get for the series.

chat.author: John Scudder
chat.text: Right. But if I remember 2026 correctly if you get enough implementations later and there are no other issues that arise (say, another erratum) then you can reclassify the bis that was published as PS.

chat.author: Jeffrey Haas
chat.text: Understood. My point is it's "silly" to do a bfd standard that can't include the base doc.
